topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, aged paper felt alien beneath my fingertips. I traced the elegant script detailing my life—my triumphs, my supposed sorrows, the very texture of my existence—and a cold dread began to coil in my stomach. Every carefully chosen anecdote, every significant turning point, felt like a borrowed costume. I reread the final paragraph, the one describing my 'quiet ascent to prominence,' and a tremor ran through my hands. How could someone have shaped this narrative so completely, so flawlessly, without ever consulting me? I sat motionless in the quiet library, the silence amplifying the frantic hammering of my heart against my ribs. I started pacing the length of the room, my breath catching in shallow gasps, the conviction that I was fundamentally misplaced settling heavily upon my shoulders. I needed to find the author, needed to confront the deception, but the sheer weight of the fabrication made my limbs feel heavy, rooted to the spot.

[story 2]

When the documents arrived, the world seemed to tilt on its axis. I stared at the meticulously crafted timeline, the dates, the quoted sentiments—they were all *mine*, yet they didn't resonate with the messy, unpredictable reality of my memory. A wave of intense shame washed over me, leaving a clammy sheen on my palms. I crumpled the pages slowly, the sound of the tearing paper shockingly loud in the otherwise hushed apartment. I walked to the window, watching the city lights blur, feeling utterly exposed. My jaw tightened so hard I could taste copper. I spent the next hour scrubbing furiously at the desk, trying to erase the evidence, an absurd, futile gesture. Every time I looked at my reflection, the unfamiliarity of the polished story reflected back, and I felt a profound sense of wrongness, a deep, internal churning that made breathing difficult.

[story 3]

The revelation hit me like a physical blow during the late afternoon light filtering through the blinds. I was holding the manuscript, the words detailing my entire trajectory laid bare, and a strange, hollow ache settled deep within my chest. My gaze kept darting back and forth across the text, searching for a flaw, a misplaced comma that would betray the deception. When I finally forced myself to read the section describing my pivotal decision, a sharp, involuntary shudder ran through my frame. It was a profound sense of betrayal, not just toward the writer, but toward the self I thought I knew. I leaned back in my chair, letting the silence stretch taut between us, my shoulders slumping in defeat. The need to explain, to demand accountability, warred fiercely with a paralyzing reluctance to move, as if any action would only confirm the depth of the intricate falsehood.
